item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: The image that was used in this article is non-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0) as the image comes from here and is Copyrighted. Thus, the image has been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yeah, the writing is good, but the irony here is just delicious.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The addendum about the library fires is a wonderful twist. At first you think "well, they're books about the evils of sharing knowledge; obviously they hate their existence and want to burn as soon as they're not doing their job"… and then you read that someone gave copies to libraries, and the self-immolation goes from "suicide" to "suicide bombing."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
written in an Eastern Slavic language

The book on the picture is in German. Something along the lines of "A small textbook for the young generation". You may want to pick another language group.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This might be the first SCP I've read which used the "it's mind-affected, but if you don't use it, it becomes more dangerous" aspect in a way that actually made sense in the context of the item.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Only for brief periods when the vacuum chambers aren't viable. It's that or possibly have the damn things catch on fire in the middle of moving them from one place to another! =P

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So, you're telling me that this set of books, which already have significant water damage and general degradation, and part of it's containment procedures includes exposing it to very high humidity?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A treatise against the acquisition and storage of knowledge in written form, written by a narcissist. This is amazing, especially the bit where it bursts into flame whenever it encounters anything that doesn't conform to its view of how the world should be. You've somehow managed to distill Narcissistic Personality Disorder into an SCP. I am really, really impressed. And I'm a librarian and the mere idea of burning books is pretty horrible to me.

Upvoting!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Now updating, was not aware of the term, thank you.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like it, though just a quick point: "yellowing" is probably not the most scientific term used to describe the aging of a book. "Foxing" is the official term to describe the aging, browny, mouldy effects on an old book.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Never.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Okay let me rephrase: barring cross-SCP usage (which is thoroughly frowned upon) OR somehow stumbling across some very old backup records that *weren't* burned by these things or someone influenced by them. Happy? ;P

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What I was trying to get at was that it was able to be translated, but is rather badly written. Kind of like hearing someone with a really thick accent that uses a lot of local colloquialisms; you get the jist of most of what they're saying, but then they come out with something like "Me'n mates waffed th'keeg's barrah o'er night," and you're not sure if he's talking about a pub crawl with his buddies, or if they visited a certain part of town, or what.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Sure there are ways for the Foundation to learn this. At least two SCPs — SCP-028 andSCP-374 — are general-knowledge oracles, and could hypothetically explain the book. I'm sure SCP-738 could provide the information as well — for a ghastly hideous price, of course.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I rather enjoy this concept, but I have one minor quibble:

an Eastern Slavic language, apparently of the early-to-mid 1800's and possibly an obscure local dialect or mixture of languages.

This (to me) is implying that we can't really read the language(s) it's written in, yet you go on to give a fairly detailed synopsis of what it's saying.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
That's actually just about what I had in my head! The person doing it was a definite subscriber to the theory of "Knowledge is power; guard it well", and probably would have only even written the simple premise to spread it more easily, a necessary evil from his/her standpoint. Of course, being a complete narcissist, the simple premise ended up becoming a rant spanning four hardbound volumes. Somewhere along the line, the insanity of it all twisted up the very rules of the universe and the books became the embodiement of the philosophy itself, burning all other sources of knowledge and leaving only itself intact.

…. Of course there's literally no possible way for the Foundation to acquire this information beyond what may have been written in the books, so it won't turn up in any official statements, and thus shouldn't bind anyone else's head-canon ;)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not anyone in particular, I was just thinking about when commercial printing presses were becoming available, and for some reason "Eastern Europe" was stuck in my head. I was going to try to have it related to various instances of book burnings and other public riots of the like, but I didn't want to end up bloating it too badly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Self-loathing SCPs are the best SCPs. It's kind of fun trying to picture the person who wrote this, shaking his fists in frustration at the thought of all that knowledge filtering down to people who didn't deserve it, running out to the printer's to self-publish his treatise against the publishing of treatises. I like to think that the origins of the books themselves were entirely mundane; it was the subsequent irony singularity that made them anomalous.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Anti-book books which burn other books. I like it.

Also, I'm curious: were there any politicians, philosophers or political movements in the early to mid 1800's which inspired this?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like this version. First they try to convince you that you are right, then they try to destroy what they are against by fire. They're the suicide bombers of knowledge.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Gladly shifting my vote from -1 to +1.

Nice work!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Tried to neaten it up a bit more, be a bit clearer on certain things, also put in the vacuum chambers. Honestly, looking at it makes sense to me, if anyone sees something that's particularly bugging them or could be worded better that my mind won't work out right now, feel free to message me or post here with specifics, or at least ask if it's okay to put it in yourself.

(Spoiler: It's probably going to be okay)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: …. I'll be dead honest I forgot about the chamber concept. I think I'll combine the two, stating those conditions must be met IF the books must be transferred out of said chambers for whatever reason (sample procurement, microscopic evaluation, etc)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I believe almost everything can be fixed giving it a good proofreading when you get home.

The containment procedure could be changed to something simpler and more energy efficient.

For now you're keeping it in a room that must have its moisture and temperature controlled so the books won't self-immolate, but it would make a lot more sense if it was kept in an anaerobic chamber, preventing immolation and providing a very secure way for manipulating this SCP.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was actively trying to not make it memetic, had to fight an impulse to make it turn people into street-corner-raving maniacs whose speeches would "infect" others, leading to huge book-burning/teacher-assaulting mobs.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That particular instance was me fixing a typo and not reworking something else; was actually supposed to read as "… written texts, and areas which house them, especially libraries." As stated above I'll be going over it a bit more closely later tonight.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Suddenly getting barraged with stuff at work, so editing for clunkiness will have to wait till I get home some time tonight. Any suggestions on particular things you'd like to see addressed?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is a good example of a mind effecting object that is not memetic.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Pretty cool, guy. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The self-immolation thing feels unconnected to the rest, but this is otherwise kind of interesting. Main issue is word agreement (libraries, for example, are not written texts).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I agree with that.

Keeping it at -1 for now, willing to change it to +1 if these issues are fixed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Basically, even though it can't really burn itself, so it's more like "Fun, you pathetic humans, if you won't burn down all knowledge I WILL!"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A treatise against the dispersal of knowledge that self-immolates when left alone. Huh. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I almost really like this. I think the wording of the containment procedures is clunky and could be rewritten. I do like that it's been causing havok in Eastern Europe—it's kind of like a self-hating book if you think about it. It makes people hate learning and if left alone long enough tries to commit suicide XD

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Posted up as part of Bright's 24-hour challenge.
Actually inspired by a typo made in another object's discussion, talking about humidity being kept at all "tomes" instead of "times."
You probably don't even want to know the train of thought that followed which lead to this.
